nameMeanings.name: Rishton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: English:  see  Rushton  . Early bearers: Aleander Rishton, 1560 in   IGI   (Great Harwood, Lancs); Anna Rishton, 1598, Elizabeth Rushton, 1635 in   IGI   (Altham, Lancs); Robert Rishton, 1603, James Rushton, 1627, Robert Riston, 1757 in   IGI   (Colne, Lancs).

variants.name: Rushton
meanings: English: habitational name from Rishton (Lancashire) and Rushton (Staffordshire), and perhaps also from Rushton (Cheshire, Northamptonshire), Rushton in East Stoke (Dorset), and Rhiston (Shropshire). The placenames derive from Old English risc ‘rush’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’.

variants.name: IGI
meanings: 1 English: (i) perhaps a variant of Garth with loss of -r- before the final consonant. Compare Thomas Gath, 1600, John Garth, 1619 in IGI (Saint Keyne, Cornwall); Nicholas Gath, 1716, Thomas Garth, 1726 in IGI (Saint Breward, Cornwall). (ii) alternatively, a variant of Goth with unrounding of the vowel, if not vice versa.

variants.name: Riston
meanings: English: habitational name from Long Riston (East Yorkshire), North and South Reston (Lincolnshire), and East and Sco Ruston (Norfolk). The placenames derive from Old English hrīs ‘brushwood’ + tūn ‘farmstead, estate’. The name may sometimes have been confused with Ritson .
Scottish: habitational name from Reston in Coldingham (Berwickshire), cognate with 1 above.

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .

nameMeanings.name: de
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.

historicEvents.title: Jews Expelled From England
historicEvents.description: After years of anti-Semitic behavior, King Edward I expelled Jews from England in 1290.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

en.lifeSummary: When Robert de Rishton was born about 1270, in Rishton, Lancashire, England, his father, Gilbert de Rishton, was 27 and his mother, Mrs Gilbert Rishton, was 27. He had at least 1 son.
